chain_of_thought: Allocator frame is __memcpy; first app-level native frame below it is Init_SEED/SEED_CBC/EncCertExchangeMessage_to_CertExchangeMessage/DecryptCertExchange in libUbikeyJni.so, indicating a cryptographic SEED CBC implementation copying attacker-influenced data.
Crash in __memcpy strongly suggests out-of-bounds read/write or invalid pointer, not a deliberate abort; typical in symmetric cipher code if buffer length or padding is mishandled.

Native stack shows sequence Init_SEED -> SEED_CBC -> EncCertExchangeMessage_to_CertExchangeMessage -> DecryptCertExchange -> Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt, tying the crash to decryption of a 'CertExchange' message structure.

JNI bridge exposed to Java as com.ntiusp.pushagent.bridge.apis.JniInterfaces.jni_crypto_decrypt(byte[]):byte[]; JNI likely forwards byte[] directly into UbikeyJni_jdecrypt without strong size validation beyond array length.

Java call graph indicates many entrypoints from service/receiver/onReceive/onStartCommand paths, which are driven by Intents and network push agent logic; thus decrypted data is realistically derived from untrusted or semi-trusted remote payloads or server responses.

The fuzz harness fuzz_one_input replaces the real Java caller but uses the same JNI bridge signature and libUbikeyJni routines, so the crash mechanism is representative of what could happen with malformed ciphertext from remote sources.

SEED_CBC and EncCertExchangeMessage_to_CertExchangeMessage likely perform memcpy into stack/heap buffers sized based on decrypted length fields or fixed struct size; if upstream treats incoming ciphertext length as trustworthy or miscomputes decrypted/plaintext size, __memcpy can overrun buffers.

Absence of allocator/sanitizer abort frames suggests raw SIGSEGV or similar in memcpy, consistent with invalid dest/src pointer or oversized length, which is typically exploitable memory corruption if attacker can influence length/content.

Given crypto context, attacker may not fully control plaintext, but they do control ciphertext length and bits; many padding/length parsing bugs become reachable with chosen ciphertexts, especially if no authenticated encryption is enforced, so this can at minimum be a robust DoS and potentially memory corruption.

lib_map confirms UbikeyJni.so exports SEED_CBC, EncCertExchangeMessage_to_CertExchangeMessage, DecryptCertExchange, and JNI decrypt, indicating app-level code rather than harness-only or third-party allocator issue.

No evidence in provided data that JNI or Java perform strict bounds checks on the provided byte[] beyond normal array semantics; no fixed maximum length or validation before passing into native decryption routines is indicated.

Therefore, the most reasonable classification is a likely vulnerability: attacker-controllable or semi-controllable ciphertext leading to unsafe memcpy in SEED_CBC/DecryptCertExchange, causing potential out-of-bounds access (CWE-787) and app crash (at least DoS, possibly more if exploited carefully).
reasons: Crash occurs in __memcpy invoked from custom SEED_CBC/DecryptCertExchange code in libUbikeyJni.so, indicating likely buffer overrun or invalid pointer rather than benign abort.

JNI method jni_crypto_decrypt(byte[]) directly exposes native decryption to Java, with many call paths from services/receivers handling Intents and push-related data, so attacker-influenced input can reach the vulnerable memcpy.

Fuzz harness uses the same JNI entrypoint and native code, and triggers memcpy crash without any sanitizer/allocator wrapper, consistent with an underlying memory safety bug in the crypto/decode routines.

evidence.snippet: JNI entry for decryption, called as com.ntiusp.pushagent.bridge.apis.JniInterfaces.jni_crypto_decrypt(byte[]):byte[]; forwards Java byte[] into native SEED-based decryption pipeline.
evidence.note: Native decrypt pipeline reachable from multiple service/receiver paths per Java call graph, indicating remote/control-plane data can reach it.

evidence.snippet: Call chain immediately below __memcpy at crash site, showing custom SEED CBC and certificate-exchange-message parsing around memcpy.
evidence.note: Mis-sized buffer or length miscalculation in this chain would lead directly to memcpy overflow/underflow.

recommendations: Manually reverse/decompile libUbikeyJni.so, focusing on DecryptCertExchange, EncCertExchangeMessage_to_CertExchangeMessage, and SEED_CBC to identify all memcpy/memmove calls and verify length and buffer-size calculations.
Add strict bounds checks in the JNI decrypt path (jni_crypto_decrypt/UbikeyJni_jdecrypt) to enforce a maximum accepted ciphertext length and validate any decoded length fields before memcpy.

Introduce authenticated encryption or integrity checks for the CertExchange messages; reject malformed or tampered ciphertexts before attempting decryption or structure parsing to prevent attacker-controlled crashes.
assumptions: jni_crypto_decrypt(byte[]) forwards data with minimal or no size validation to Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt.
Incoming data to jni_crypto_decrypt in production can originate from network/push/intent payloads and is not fully trusted.
The crash in __memcpy is due to out-of-bounds access or invalid pointer rather than a deliberate abort inside memcpy.
limitations: Ghidra MCP access to libUbikeyJni.so failed, so memcpy argument expressions and exact buffer sizes could not be inspected directly.
Jadx MCP could not provide full Java source for all callgraph nodes, so exact validation logic (if any) on the ciphertext length is unknown.
No concrete register/memory dump around the crash was available to prove whether the overflow is read vs write or exact controllability of the length.

chain_of_thought: Tooling is unavailable (connection refused), so I must reason using only the textual crash entry and general reverse‑engineering knowledge.

The native stack shows a crash (process termination) in Get_DER_Child, called from Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage → DecryptCertExchange → Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt → fuzz_one_input → main. Get_DER_Child and the other functions are all in libUbikeyJni.so, which is an app-provided native library, not a system allocator/sanitizer. So the first application-level native frame is Get_DER_Child itself.

The function name Get_DER_Child and its callers Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage / DecryptCertExchange strongly suggest manual parsing of ASN.1/DER-encoded certificate exchange messages. Such parsers are classic sources of memory safety bugs (out-of-bounds reads/writes, length miscalculations, etc.) if length fields are not validated carefully.

The termination reason is only “Process Termination : Get_DER_Child”; no explicit sanitizer (ASan/Scudo) or malloc/free call is in the top frames. That could mean an explicit abort/LOG_FATAL inside Get_DER_Child in response to malformed input, or a crash (SIGSEGV) that the triage system summarized as process termination. Without symbols/assembly I cannot distinguish, but given the function naming it is likely that malformed ASN.1 structures trigger an error path or a bug.

Next, analyze the JNI entry. The stack shows Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt in libUbikeyJni.so, which is almost certainly the native implementation backing some UbikeyJni.jdecrypt Java method. However, the recorded JNI Bridge Method for the fuzz harness is com.ntiusp.pushagent.bridge.apis.JniInterfaces.jni_crypto_decrypt([B)[B. That suggests the fuzz harness stubs jni_crypto_decrypt and underneath routes to UbikeyJni.jdecrypt, or that the app wraps Ubikey’s JNI function in its own bridge. In any case, from the Java call graph we see how arbitrary Java byte[] data reach the native decryption routine.

The Java call graph shows many different app-level entrypoints that eventually call jni_crypto_decrypt([B)[B: service onStartCommand, broadcast receiver onReceive with Intents, various run() methods, constructors that may execute at startup, etc. Upstream of these, com.ntiusp.pushagent.a.a.c([B)[B is the original method taking a byte[]; this likely is some high-level crypto/decode entry that hands data to the JNI bridge. Because some of these paths originate from Intents and network-ish agent services, there is a plausible path from attacker-controlled or semi-untrusted data (push messages, NTP agent messages, broadcast intents) to the JNI decryption method, meaning the ASN.1 data processed by Get_DER_Child can be influenced by an attacker under realistic conditions.

The central question: is the crash evidence of an unsafe condition (memory corruption / exploitable bug) versus a benign, defensive abort? With no decompiled code, I must infer from naming and typical patterns: (1) Get_DER_Child sounds like a helper to walk children in a DER-encoded ASN.1 sequence. Such helpers commonly take a pointer and length, then step through TLV entries. If length checks are missing or off-by-one, out-of-bounds read/write or invalid pointer dereferences occur when iterating malformed input supplied via the certificate exchange message. (2) The function Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage very likely builds a structured object from raw ASN.1: again, complex loops / pointer arithmetic likely. (3) Fuzzing specifically targeted jdecrypt with arbitrary data, and this specific Get_DER_Child crash was discovered, implying the implementation misbehaved under malformed inputs. Well-designed libraries typically treat malformed ASN.1 as parse errors without crashing, returning an error code; a process termination in such a helper strongly hints at a bug instead of just a clean error return.

The triage description does not say this is an assertion failure with a clear message; it only says Process Termination : Get_DER_Child. Some vendors indeed call abort() or exit() on parse errors, but that is poor practice and uncommon on mobile; and if so, this is at least a denial-of-service vulnerability if an attacker can feed malformed data that reach this code path (e.g., malicious server / MITM during certificate-based protocols).

Considering the app context: kr.co.busanbank.mbp and UbikeyJni.so likely involve mobile banking and token / key management. DecryptCertExchange suggests certificate-based key exchange, perhaps with a remote server. If an attacker can control those messages (e.g., through network, MITM, or a compromised server), they can send malformed ASN.1 that cause Get_DER_Child to crash – at minimum a remote DoS against the banking app’s secure channel or login process. Depending on the exact bug (bounds mischeck), there may be potential for more severe memory corruption exploits, but I cannot confirm without code.

Next, evaluate whether this might be a harness-only artefact: fuzz_one_input is clearly from the fuzzer, but the call sequence jdecrypt → DecryptCertExchange → Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage → Get_DER_Child is exactly the normal code path for parsing a certificate exchange message in production, not a special harness stub. The harness merely passes arbitrary bytes into the same JNI entry. So the path is realistic, and the same kind of malformed input could appear over the network or from an attacker impersonating the remote endpoint. This is not something that requires the harness to pass impossible types or violate calling conventions; it is just supplying bad ciphertext/ASN.1 bytes.

I must also consider if strong Java/JNI validation might prevent extreme cases in production. The Java call graph only indicates that a byte[] is passed into jni_crypto_decrypt; there is no evidence that the Java side enforces strict length limits or validates ASN.1 structure before invoking JNI, because the actual intentions of methods like com.ntiusp.pushagent.b.f.a.b(String) and com.ntiusp.pushagent.c.e.c()/d()/e()/f() are unknown. Given typical design, they likely handle configuration and message wrapping, not deep ASN.1 validation; validation is left to the native crypto library. Therefore I assume no robust mitigation at the Java layer against malformed ASN.1 lengths, especially under complex network/intent inputs.

Given all of this, I assess that there is at least a genuine, reachable denial-of-service vulnerability, quite possibly backed by unsafe memory handling (length / bounds errors) inside Get_DER_Child or its callers. However, lacking concrete disassembly, I cannot firmly assert memory corruption or RCE. Thus the safest classification is “vulnerable (DoS / potential memory safety issues)” with medium-to-high severity, moderate-high confidence (since fuzzing already triggered a process crash with attacker-like inputs).

Without Ghidra, I cannot construct an exact call_sequence of internal helper functions, but from the stack we know: JNI entry: Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt → DecryptCertExchange → Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage → Get_DER_Child. JNI bridge from the Java side is com.ntiusp.pushagent.bridge.apis.JniInterfaces.jni_crypto_decrypt([B)[B. I will report that path.

For CWE classification, this looks like improper input validation in a cryptographic message parser that can lead to abnormal termination; minimum: CWE-400 (Uncontrolled Resource Consumption / DoS) or CWE-20 (Improper Input Validation). If we treat the root cause as a likely bounds issue in ASN.1 parsing, CWE-125 (Out-of-bounds Read) or CWE-787 (Out-of-bounds Write). Given uncertainty, include CWE-20 and CWE-400; possibly CWE-125 as a likely mechanism.
I’ll set severity to medium (since it is at least app DoS, in a sensitive security context). If later code inspection confirms memory corruption, severity could be raised to high/critical.
reasons: Crash occurs inside app’s ASN.1/DER parsing helpers (Get_DER_Child → Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage → DecryptCertExchange) rather than in the fuzzer harness or system allocators, indicating an app-level bug.

Java call graph shows realistic paths from service/broadcast/agent logic into jni_crypto_decrypt([B)[B, so attacker-controlled or network-derived data can influence the ASN.1 bytes passed into the vulnerable native functions.

Well-designed ASN.1 parsers should return errors on malformed input, not terminate the process; a process termination in Get_DER_Child strongly suggests unsafe handling (bounds/length mischecks) or at least a remotely triggerable denial-of-service.

evidence.note: Top native frame where process terminates while parsing ASN.1/DER structures for certificate exchange.
Called before Get_DER_Child, indicates complex parsing of ASN.1 EncCertExchangeMessage, a typical source of length/bounds bugs.
JNI bridge receiving a byte[] from multiple app entrypoints (service, receiver, runnables), propagating potentially attacker-controlled data into the native decryption/parsing code.

recommendations: Manually audit Get_DER_Child, Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage, and DecryptCertExchange for length/bounds checks on ASN.1/DER parsing; ensure all offsets and lengths are validated against the buffer size before use.
Replace any abort()/exit() or unchecked pointer arithmetic inside the ASN.1 parsing logic with robust error handling that returns failure without crashing the process.
Add fuzz testing with structured and malformed ASN.1 inputs for the jdecrypt/jni_crypto_decrypt entrypoint, and fix any further crashes or sanitizer findings (ASan/UBSan) revealed during testing.
assumptions: Malformed ASN.1/certificate exchange data can be influenced by a remote party (e.g., server, MITM, or crafted push/agent messages) such that it reaches jni_crypto_decrypt([B)[B.
The process termination observed is due to a bug or unsafe handling in Get_DER_Child or its callers, not a deliberate, fully specified design choice to abort the app on parse errors.
limitations: Ghidra/JADX MCP tools were unavailable, so the native and Java code for Get_DER_Child and jni_crypto_decrypt could not be inspected directly.
Exact crash mechanism (out-of-bounds access vs. explicit abort/assert) is unknown; classification is based on naming, control flow, and typical patterns rather than direct disassembly.

crash.ProgramEntry: cktrace stopped: previous frame identical to this frame
StackTrace: cktrace stopped: previous frame identical to this frame

chain_of_thought: Tools are unavailable for native decompilation, so I must reason from the crash signature and metadata only.
Top native frame before abort is __stack_chk_fail, suggesting a stack canary check failure (classic stack buffer overflow or stack smashing).
The preceding frame is Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage in libUbikeyJni.so, a non-system library, so this is application/vendor crypto code, not the allocator or sanitizer.
__stack_chk_fail is called automatically on detecting a stack buffer overwrite, which is strong evidence of memory corruption (likely out-of-bounds write on stack).

The function name Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage implies it parses ASN.1-formatted data into some internal message struct; ASN.1 decoding is historically bug-prone and usually works directly over attacker-influenced input lengths and fields.
The JNI entry actually used at the native level for this library is Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt; the app’s jni_crypto_decrypt likely wraps or forwards to this function in libUbikeyJni.so.
There is no sign that this is a deliberate abort (e.g., explicit input validation); instead, it's a compiler-inserted stack canary trip, so the crash stems from overwriting the stack frame.

Java side call graph shows jni_crypto_decrypt([B)[B is called in many network/IPC/intent-driven paths (service, receiver, background runnables), indicating it processes data originating from remote push agent / bank backend or intents that could be attacker-controlled or at least semi-trusted.

The fuzz harness likely passed malformed or extreme-length ciphertext or ASN.1 blobs into jni_crypto_decrypt, exercising a latent bug in Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage; even if production traffic is normally well-formed, lack of robust bounds checking in ASN.1 decoding remains a vulnerability (can be abused by a malicious server or MITM).

Since __stack_chk_fail is triggered, this is not just a graceful parse failure but actual memory corruption, which in principle could be exploitable for code execution, though modern mitigations (stack canaries, ASLR, NX) raise difficulty.

We lack direct decompiled code or exact unsafe call sites (memcpy, etc.), but the combination of stack canary abort in application crypto parsing, plus direct use of attacker-influenced byte[] from Java without visible validation, is strong evidence of a genuine memory safety bug.
There is no sign that the harness used unrealistic calling conventions (JNI signature matches; parameter is a simple byte array that Java can construct arbitrarily).

Therefore, I classify this crash as a likely real vulnerability: stack-based buffer overflow within Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage reachable via jni_crypto_decrypt with attacker-controlled ciphertext/ASN.1 data.

Severity: high, as this is stack corruption in a banking app’s crypto library reachable from network or push channels; while direct RCE is not confirmed, it is plausible and at minimum a robust DoS and potential info leak/exploit primitive.
Confidence is somewhat below 0.9 because we cannot inspect the actual implementation to see the specific unsafe operation or the exact trust boundary, but the evidence is still quite strong.
reasons: Crash occurs in application crypto function Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage immediately before __stack_chk_fail, indicating stack buffer overflow or stack smashing in vendor code.
JNI bridge jni_crypto_decrypt([B)[B accepts arbitrary byte[] from Java and is used along remote/intent-driven paths, so ASN.1 input is at least partially attacker-controlled.
Abort is caused by compiler-inserted stack canary, not an intentional sanity check, so this represents real memory corruption rather than a benign error path.

evidence.snippet: Called directly before __stack_chk_fail; function name and position indicate ASN.1 parsing into internal cert exchange structure.
evidence.note: Likely stack-based buffer overflow while converting ASN.1 input from attacker-controlled byte array.
JNI entry that forwards unvalidated byte[] to native decryption/ASN.1 logic.

call_sequence: com.ntiusp.pushagent.c.e.[c/d/e/f]()Ljava/lang/String; or various service/receiver run() and onReceive/onStartCommand paths
recommendations: Reverse-engineer Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage in libUbikeyJni.so to locate stack-based buffers and ensure all ASN.1 length fields are bounds-checked before copying or writing.
Add strict size and structural validation of ciphertext/ASN.1 blobs in both Java and JNI layers before passing them into the ASN.1 conversion routine.
Rebuild libUbikeyJni.so with full compiler hardening (stack canaries, -fstack-clash-protection, _FORTIFY_SOURCE, RELRO) and add regression tests with malformed ASN.1 inputs to prevent recurrence.
assumptions: jni_crypto_decrypt ultimately calls Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt in libUbikeyJni.so.
ASN.1 data decoded by Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage is derived from network/push or otherwise attacker-controlled input.
The crash was not induced by an artificial harness-only misuse (e.g., passing null pointers outside of what Java can normally create).
limitations: Native library implementation could not be decompiled due to tool connectivity issues, so specific unsafe calls (memcpy, etc.) are inferred.
Exact trust boundary and format of the byte[] passed to jni_crypto_decrypt are unknown; assessment assumes it can be influenced by remote data.
Exploitability beyond denial-of-service (e.g., reliable RCE) not confirmed without deeper binary analysis and runtime context.

chain_of_thought: Top native frame below runtime is Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage inside libUbikeyJni.so, followed by DecryptCertExchange and the JNI function Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt. The immediate termination is abort via __stack_chk_fail, which is triggered by a stack canary check failure. This almost always indicates a stack-based buffer overflow (write past end of a local buffer or corrupted saved frame data) in Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage or a function it calls, not an intentional logical abort.

Because tools are unavailable, I cannot decompile the function, but the symbol name strongly implies ASN.1 parsing and conversion into an encrypted certificate exchange message: such code typically parses variable-length ASN.1 elements from attacker-influenced data into fixed-size local structures or stack buffers. A stack canary failure during this conversion is highly consistent with out-of-bounds writes due to insufficient bounds checking on ASN.1 lengths or overall input size.

The JNI entry point is Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jdecrypt in libUbikeyJni.so. The JniInterfaces.jni_crypto_decrypt([B)[B bridge likely wraps this JNI function (or another decrypt wrapper that calls it). The fuzzer harness fuzz_one_input clearly controls the byte[] passed into jni_crypto_decrypt. In real use, the Java call graph shows many paths from push agent service/receiver and worker runnables, where the decrypt is applied to strings or byte arrays presumably derived from network push payloads or server messages, i.e., attacker-controlled data over the network, not fixed constants.

There is no evidence of strict size limits or ASN.1 validation in the Java paths: the input byte[] is passed down into native without length checks. The native library, being a crypto / certificate handler, likely expects well-formed ASN.1; malformed or oversized ASN.1 elements can trigger length miscalculations. Combined with the stack canary failure, this makes a real stack-based buffer overflow in Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage very plausible and not just a benign assert.

The abort is not clearly a deliberate application-level abort (e.g., calling abort on validation failure); instead, it comes from __stack_chk_fail, which is injected by the compiler when a stack canary is corrupted. This directly indicates memory corruption in native code. Since this is reachable via JNI and the Java graph shows realistic app paths (push messages, service commands, receivers), an external attacker controlling the decrypted data (or its ASN.1-encoded form) could realistically trigger the same condition without a fuzz harness.

Therefore, this crash should be treated as a likely exploitable memory corruption (stack-based buffer overflow) vulnerability in libUbikeyJni.so, with network-reachable inputs. Even without full decompilation, the combination of function naming, stack canary failure, attacker-controlled input path, and the fact that this is certificate/ASN.1 parsing code strongly supports classification as a genuine vulnerability, with at least high severity (possible RCE or at minimum reliable native process crash).
reasons: __stack_chk_fail indicates stack canary corruption, consistent with stack-based buffer overflow in Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage
Input to the vulnerable code originates from a JNI-decrypt path that accepts attacker-controllable byte[] (fuzzer and likely network push data) without obvious Java-side bounds checks

The vulnerable function name and context (ASN.1 to certificate exchange conversion) implies complex parsing of variable-length data, a common source of overflow bugs, and the crash occurs during that conversion

evidence.note: Stack canary failure in ASN.1 conversion routine suggests stack-based buffer overflow while handling ASN.1 input.

evidence.snippet: Java call graph shows multiple real app paths leading to jni_crypto_decrypt([B)[B from services/receivers handling push/intent data.
evidence.note: Indicates network/IPC-derived attacker-controlled input reaches the native decrypt routine.

recommendations: Reverse-engineer and audit Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage and related ASN.1 parsing code for all variable-length fields; ensure strict bounds checking before copying into stack or fixed-size buffers.

Add robust ASN.1 and length validation in native code (and optionally Java) before decrypt/convert operations; reject malformed or oversized messages gracefully without memory writes beyond buffer limits.
Rebuild libUbikeyJni.so with security hardening (full RELRO, stack protector, PIE, fortify) and deploy a patched library after fixing the parsing bug.
assumptions: The byte[] passed into jni_crypto_decrypt can be influenced by remote push messages or server data in production.
There is no comprehensive size/structure validation on ASN.1 input before Convert_ASN1_to_EncCertExchangeMessage operates on it.
The abort is triggered solely by stack protector due to a write beyond the local buffer bounds, not by any explicit call to abort in the app.
limitations: Unable to decompile libUbikeyJni.so in this environment to inspect exact buffer sizes and copy operations.
No direct view of Java implementation of JniInterfaces.jni_crypto_decrypt or surrounding validation logic.
Exact exploitability (code execution vs. crash-only) cannot be confirmed without more detailed binary analysis and runtime context.
